data.title: Pfizer: The Great Healthcare Plan Does Not Change My Bullish Stance
data.description:
I only own a handful of healthcare stocks. Given my preference to invest in small-cap stocks, most of these stocks are very thinly covered even on Seeking Alpha, which, in my opinion, is the best place on the Internet for small-cap research. Pfizer, Inc. (PFE) is one of the two big pharma stocks I own. The other one is Vertex Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (VRTX).

PFE stock has gained some momentum in the recent past, but a major breakthrough to the upside has not occurred yet. When I first invested in PFE, I thought the market was underestimating the company's potential to cut back on large investments to drive earnings higher. This was more than 2 years ago. I have added to my position at regular intervals since then, but I am hardly at the breakeven point with my PFE investment still.

On January 15, President Trump introduced a new healthcare framework to lower healthcare costs in the U.S., named the Great Healthcare Plan. This reminds us that the threat of adverse policy developments is far from over. However, as I discuss in this analysis, I believe Pfizer has what it takes to come out unscathed from these policy pressures and thrive in the long run.

A Closer Look At The Great Healthcare Plan

If you are a pharma investor, keeping a close eye on new regulatory developments is key, especially today, given the rapid pace at which new reforms are introduced. In the first year in Office, President Trump has already signed 2 impactful reforms into law. I am not planning to discuss these reforms in detail here, but I thought it best to summarize the potential impact of these 2 reforms on Pfizer's financial performance.

Policy reform Comments Impact on Pfizer
Medicare Part D redesign under the Inflation Reduction Act Drug manufacturers are now liable to offer a 20% discount on brand-name drugs in the catastrophic phase of coverage. Pfizer management has guided for a $1 billion impact from this reform in 2025 alone.
The One Big Beautiful Act The corporate effective tax rate was lowered by this bill. This is a tailwind for Pfizer. In Q3, the company reported a negative effective tax rate.

Source: Company filings

In addition to these major reforms, I believe PFE investors need to keep a close eye on ongoing Section 232 investigations as well.

Coming back to the Great Healthcare Plan, this framework was introduced to achieve 4 main objectives. You can read the full text here.

  1. Lower drug prices.
  2. Lower insurance premiums.
  3. Hold insurance companies accountable on all fronts.
  4. Maximize drug price transparency.

In the next segment, I will discuss why Pfizer is in a strong position to weather the negative impact from these policy changes and thrive in the long run.

The Great Healthcare Plan Will Not Push Pfizer Into A Corner

For Pfizer, the most direct negative impact will arise from the Trump administration's efforts to lower drug prices. As part of the Great Healthcare Plan, the goal is to ensure Americans get the same prescription drug prices offered to people in other countries. In a nutshell, Pfizer (and other drug manufacturers) will have a ceiling on drug prices. The company's pricing power will no longer be as strong as it has been in the past. While this is not an encouraging development, pharma investors have to deal with this reality going forward.

The Great Healthcare Plan's objective to hold insurance companies accountable on all fronts could actually turn out to be a net positive for Pfizer. It is standard practice for a drug maker to increase the list prices of drugs to cover the substantial kickbacks (in the form of rebates) to pharmacy benefit managers. The Great Healthcare Plan aims to end kickbacks from PBMs. If this is achieved, Pfizer could potentially lower drug prices to meet the government's demands without actually having to dent its revenue materially. The Great Healthcare Plan's stated goal is to hold insurance companies accountable and therefore could actually turn out to be a hedge for the threat posed by lower drug prices. I will monitor the pharma space closely to see how the Trump administration moves forward to bring the Great Healthcare Plan alive, to evaluate if Pfizer could benefit from restrictions on insurance companies.

The Great Healthcare Plan also includes an interesting statement: voluntarily negotiated deals with HHS/CMS will be grandfathered in.

Last September, Pfizer reached an agreement with the Trump administration to lower drug prices voluntarily, making it the first pharma company to voluntarily negotiate a deal with the government. This agreement covers almost all the requirements highlighted by President Trump in a letter in July 2025, calling for pharma companies to offer favorable drug pricing to Americans.

  1. Pfizer agreed to offer most-favored-nation pricing for its drugs in Medicaid.
  2. The company also agreed to offer discounts on direct sales of its drugs.
  3. Self-enforcing a ceiling on new drug prices, Pfizer also agreed to ensure pricing parity with developed markets for all of its new drug launches.

Although a hit on Pfizer's pricing power is unavoidable, the good news is that Pfizer has more clarity on both pricing and tariffs thanks to this voluntary agreement. In return for Pfizer agreeing to lower drug prices, the Trump administration has agreed to provide Pfizer a 3-year grace period to exempt the company from Section 232 tariffs. The catch is that Pfizer would have to invest in the U.S. to bring manufacturing onshore, which the company is already doing. In the absence of this deal, Pfizer was facing a potential 100% tariff on active pharmaceutical ingredients manufactured in its global locations.

This agreement also allows Pfizer to sell directly on TrumpRx.gov, which is expected to be launched soon. For those who are not familiar with TrumpRx.gov, this is a new platform where drug manufacturers will be able to directly sell prescription drugs to patients, bypassing PBMs (albeit at a discounted rate).

In addition to this, Pfizer's voluntary agreement makes the company immune to stricter restrictions that may be introduced under the Great Healthcare Plan in the future. This is because pharma companies that have voluntarily agreed to slash drug prices will be grandfathered in.

Bull Thesis Remains Intact

My bullish stance on Pfizer stems from the strength of the company's pipeline, the ongoing integration of its recent acquisitions (Metsera and Seagen), and its cheap valuation. I believe Mr. Market is still oblivious to how the company is setting itself up for stellar long-term earnings growth by replacing its patent-expiring blockbusters with newcomers. You can read my previous analyses for an in-depth view of my bullish stance.

In a nutshell, I believe Pfizer's current valuation is detached from the economic reality facing the company. At a forward P/E of just 8.2x (a significant discount to the sector median of ~18x), Pfizer is valued as if it is already past peak earnings, when in reality, 2026 is shaping up to be a very important year for the company from a new drug development perspective.

Key catalysts expected in 2026

You can check the full development pipeline here.

As I have discussed in my previous analyses, I believe Pfizer's development pipeline is home to a few drugs with blockbuster potential, which alleviates the threat posed by some near-term patent expirations. The good thing is that Pfizer is rewarding investors handsomely to patiently wait until this drug portfolio transformation is complete. The current dividend, which yields 6.7%, is well supported by the $4.5 billion the company saved with its cost realignment program in addition to its operating cash flows.

Although my bullish stance is unshattered by the Great Healthcare Plan, I believe investors should keep their eyes on several risks, including the threat of adverse policy developments. I am monitoring how Pfizer will be impacted by discounted drug pricing in 2026 to evaluate the impact on operating margins. I am also keeping an eye on Pfizer's U.S. investments to determine how these investments will impact ROE. In addition, I believe investors need to monitor the increased tax risk arising from onshoring manufacturing.

Q4 Earnings Preview

Pfizer is scheduled to report Q4 2025 earnings on February 3. Consensus estimates point to EPS of 57 cents for Q4 on revenue of $16.83 billion. More than the headline numbers, I believe investors should pay close attention to Pfizer's guidance for 2026. Any change to the current 2026 guidance is likely to be the biggest driver of PFE stock in the post-earnings trading session. As a long-term-oriented shareholder, I am looking forward to the upcoming earnings call where the management is likely to give color on a few important developments.

  1. Commentary on the progress of the Metsera integration. Pfizer's obesity drug plans largely revolve around Metsera.
  2. Any commentary on the expected margin impact from the voluntary agreement it reached with the government.
  3. Commentary on the plans for a resumption in buybacks.
  4. Commentary on CapEx growth to meet the requirements in its agreement with the government to bring manufacturing onshore.

Takeaway

Pfizer is one of the few big pharma companies I own. Although the Great Healthcare Plan renews fears of adverse policy developments, I believe 2026 will be a year when Mr. Market shifts its focus from regulatory developments to Pfizer's pipeline once again. When this happens, I expect long-term shareholders to be rewarded handsomely.

data.title: Why Spotify's Recent Dip Is A 'Buy' Signal For Patient Investors
data.description:
I'm evaluating Spotify Technology (SPOT) stock today. Its stock is down nearly 25% in the last six months.

So I want to get an idea of what its valuation says about expectations and how likely or not the company is to meet those.

As I outline in the paragraphs that follow, recent trends and reverse DCF modeling reveal that the company is well-positioned for long-term compounding.

Background

I'm going to start by getting an idea of how the company makes money.

So Spotify makes money via premium subscription and ad-supported revenue from its free tier. The "cost of revenues" line on its income statement is basically the "royalties bucket," which is Spotify's largest expense by far. In the third quarter, the cost of revenues made up 68.38% of total revenues. Just a couple of years ago, this figure was 73%. This is the direct cost of providing the service, and the majority of this goes to royalty payments to record labels, like Sony and Warner, publishers, and independent distributors.

So if you take the total revenues of ~$5.02 billion in Q3 and subtract the cost of revenue, Spotify's gross profit was roughly $1.59 billion. Spotify's operating income, which is the actual profit from operations after all bills are paid, was $683.3 million in Q3.

Trends

So I wanted to map this out to kind of show people that Spotify is really just working with a sliver of gross profit. In other words, it doesn't have a lot of leeway because of royalty payments. Any “operating leverage” results predominantly from revenues rising while costs like SG&A and R&D remain the same (and, thus, become a larger portion of revenues). That said, even small improvements to gross margins flow directly into the bottom line.

The assumption feasibility analysis above looks at Spotify's last 8 quarters of revenue and free cash flow margin.

So Spotify is growing its revenue by around 14.5% year-over-year. And you can also see that its free cash flow margin trend is improving as well, with a historical average of 13.6%.

You'll notice that this falls materially below the industry benchmark for technology at 22%, and this is directly related to Spotify's dependence on royalties.

Valuation

Now let's put together the discounted cash flow analysis of Spotify.

For Year 1 revenue, I went with $23.1 billion, which is the consensus analyst estimate for 2026. My estimates for revenue growth and free cash flow margin are 14% each.

Most everything else is either standard DCF methodology or was derived from Spotify's most recent financial statements.

As implied by the valuation assessment stating “overvalued by just $2.70,” these assumptions appear to be close to what Spotify is priced for.

As I often warn, 14% revenue growth year-over-year may not seem like much, but this represents a CAGR over the nine-year projection. So this actually sees the company breaching $60 billion in annual revenue by Year 9.

While apparently feasible based on historical performance, we must be mindful that maintaining this type of growth for a mature company over the next decade is likely an arduous task.

My sensitivity analysis above plays the "what-if game." For instance, what if Spotify's long-duration growth drops into the high single digits, like 8%? Well, then, its stock would appear overvalued by nearly 29%. In another example, what if Spotify is able to sustain 14% year-over-year revenue, but improve its free cash flow margin to 20%? In that situation, its stock would appear undervalued by nearly 40%.

So investors are tasked with looking at this table and thinking to themselves, what can this company realistically achieve?

Levers

For growth, one key lever remains “free → premium” conversion. Management states that 60% of Premium subscribers come from the free tier. Notably, ~39.5% of MAUs are Premium subs (a proxy for paid mix). This trend hasn’t changed in recent quarters.

Quarter MAUs (M) Premium subs (M) Ad-supported MAUs (M) Premium ARPU (€)
Q4 2023 602 236 379 4.6
Q1 2024 615 239 388 4.55
Q2 2024 626 246 393 4.62
Q3 2024 640 252 402 4.71
Q4 2024 675 263 425 4.85
Q1 2025 678 268 423 4.73
Q2 2025 696 276 433 4.57
Q3 2025 713 281 446 4.53

Now, as everyone is aware, the company is increasing its U.S. premium price to $12.99 starting in February 2026.

Here is how the monthly price bump plays out per 10 million subs:

Incremental churn Net annual revenue change for 10.0M subs ($M)
0% 120
1% 104.4
2% 88.8
3% 73.2
5% 42.1
8% -4.7
10% -35.9

Break-even incremental churn is about 7.7%. Based on ex-U.S. performance, management does not expect to see a lot of churn.

Spotify has some flexibility in margins, too, but as I alluded to earlier, its “cost of revenues” gives it limited space for improvement relative to peers. Still, I think there is enough room there to make a difference.

When you take a closer look at segment performance, you can get an idea of why the company’s interested in increasing its U.S. premium pricing. In Q3, while premium subscriber growth was 12% year-over-year, premium revenue growth was only up 9% year-over-year. Premium ARPU actually declined 4% year-over-year.

The company is also working on better yield and monetization of its ad-supported revenue. Q3 ad-supported revenue was flat (constant currency). Spotify did report ad-supported gross margin gains from greater contributions from podcasts and music. So it is setting up for a better profitability phase where margin can improve before headline ad revenue re-accelerates. But I wouldn’t expect anything big here anytime soon. Management believes the second half of 2026 will be an inflection point in advertising.

So these are all solid means through which the company can see improvement on its bottom line.

As I alluded to earlier, Spotify is also making incremental progress on gross margins. And this is mostly thanks to its marketplace strategy. Its “Discovery Mode,” for instance, allows labels to trade a portion of the royalty rate for better algorithmic placement. Moreover, Spotify’s vertical integration into spaces like podcasting and audiobooks can be higher-margin because Spotify owns or controls some distribution rights. Spotify's true moat is in its “Audio OS,” and the hope is that Spotify can evolve in a similar manner to Netflix. Of course, Netflix didn't start out owning any IP.

Final Words

To wrap up (no pun intended), Spotify is looking quite healthy and has multiple levers at its disposal to boost growth or margins. I suspect that if Spotify can keep growth at a low teens rate while nudging free cash flow margins higher via price and mix, better ad yield, and incremental monetization from verticals like audiobooks, today’s price may look very attractive in hindsight. And as I pointed out, even a few hundred basis points of sustained margin expansion would have a big impact on intrinsic value.

Net-net, I like Spotify for its potential for quality compounding (initiate ‘Buy’ rating), but am also very aware of some risks/limitations moving forward (e.g., royalty ceiling, decelerating user growth).

data.title: Elevated Silver Delivery Volume Has Metal Leaving Comex Vaults
data.description:
Originally Published on January 24, 2026

The CME Comex is the Exchange where futures are traded for gold, silver, and other commodities. The CME also allows futures buyers to turn their contracts into physical metal through delivery. You can find more detail on the CME here (e.g., vault types, major/minor months, delivery explanation, historical data, etc.).

The data below looks at contract delivery where the ownership of physical metal changes hands within CME vaults. It also shows data that details the movement of metal in and out of CME vaults. It is very possible that if there is a run on the dollar and a flight into gold, this is the data that will show early warning signs.

Gold

The chart below shows the spread between the spot price of gold and the futures contract pricing. As you can see, it blew out in 2025 (increasing contango). This created the massive arbitrage that resulted in gold traveling from London to New York. When the spread between futures and spot gets distorted, it suggests there is dislocation in the market. Spreads have never fully normalized after what happened last year.

Figure 1: Spot vs Futures

As shown below, while gold delivery volume is elevated on a historical basis, it remains below the three big minor months to start the year (Jan, Mar, May). It is just barely below the July value, but given the price increase, the notional amount delivered is much higher.

Figure 2: Recent like-month delivery volume

Below is a chart that isolates the month of January going back 30 years. January last year was still a bigger overall delivery month ($6B vs $5.1B), but given that about half as many contracts have been delivered, it shows just how much the price has surged in the last year.

Figure 3: Notional Deliveries

Net new contracts (contracts that open and settle for immediate delivery) was a modest driver. Not as large as other months, but enough to be above the median.

Figure 4: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Perhaps the most important data point is the actual metal leaving Comex vaults. When metal is “delivered,” it does not mean it’s leaving the Comex. It means that registered ownership is moving from one party to another. When the metal leaves the vault, that is where things get interesting. As shown, this year saw a major influx as the arbitrage trade took hold, but that metal was flowing back out. In the latest month, a lot of Eligible was converted to Registered to satisfy delivery demand. The Comex is not (yet?) bringing in any new metal.

Figure 5: Inventory Data

Looking ahead to the February delivery period, we see a contract that is hovering near the upper end of the range when compared to other major months.

Figure 6: Open Interest Countdown

With the massive surge in inventory, the open interest relative to physical stocks is actually lower. This shows how much metal has been added to Comex vaults last year.

Figure 7: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Bottom line, January saw elevated deliveries on a historical level, but well below the 2025 highs. The Comex vaults saw little metal arrive or leave this month, it was mainly a conversion between Eligible and Registered.

Silver

While the gold market is showing a futures price that is getting higher above the spot rate (which caused metal to move from London to New York), the silver market is experiencing the opposite problem, backwardation. The spot price is above the futures price. This is actually a bigger warning sign because it highlights the pressure being put on the physical spot market.

Market Signal: Backwardation often signals a strong immediate demand or a supply shortage in the current spot market. The market is willing to pay a premium for the asset now, as the shortage is typically expected to resolve over the long term.

While the gap has closed in recent weeks, it is still there by a few pennies.

Figure 8: Spot vs Futures

While gold delivery volume has cooled some, the silver delivery volume remains elevated, breaking a new record this month.

Figure 9: Recent like-month delivery volume

Switching to notional values and focusing on the month of January produces the chart below. Again, some of this is price appreciation, but it is also an increased appetite for deliveries. In 30 years of delivery data, the notional amount is almost 10x higher than any previous January on record. The price appreciation is not denting the demand for physical.

Figure 10: Notional Deliveries

Unlike gold, net new contracts were a major driver this month, reaching the highest ever for this point in the delivery contract.

Figure 11: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Silver-eligible inventories are being removed from the vault at a pretty rapid clip.

Figure 12: Inventory Data

Registered metal (metal available for delivery) is seeing major outflows. Since September, there has been a massive drawdown in Registered supplies. This is further evidence of strain in the physical market. Investors are taking delivery and moving it out of the vault. This will be the chart to watch in 2026.

Figure 13: Inventory Data

As we approach February delivery, the silver contract came back down and now sits in the middle of the pack. It is possible that some of these contracts rolled forward to take delivery in January.

Figure 14: Open Interest Countdown

On a relative basis, open interest is still in the middle of the pack.

Figure 15: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Conclusion

The gold market has certainly calmed down when compared to the activity earlier in 2025. That said, demand remains strong despite the all-time highs in price.

Silver is showing significant demand and metal is leaving the vault at a rapid clip. This is a market in backwardation, facing high demand, record prices, and metal leaving the vault. This is a perfect storm for silver. The technical action would still benefit from a price pullback, but this data suggests any correction should be shallow.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alphabet: Valuation Concerns + Weakening Free Cash Flow = Rating Downgrade
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. - Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) was my favorite Big Tech pick last year and the only company with a setup worthy of a Buy rating in March here. However, the quote has nearly doubled (alongside the valuation), while free cash flow is taking a hit from extensive CAPEX spending on Artificial Intelligence [AI] buildout. In a sense, Alphabet has moved from a position of undervaluation to one of full valuation.

Without a doubt, the +95% price gain since my last article has far outstripped the equivalent S&P 500 advance of +19% and been productive to own. My concern is the stock is ahead of itself by a year or two.

My current view is the rush of investor enthusiasm for shares during late 2025, including the announcement of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) had acquired a large stake, which may mean 2026 will be a year of rest for the stock. Don't get me wrong; I still like the diversification of technology holdings and all the development efforts in the works. Yet, a level-headed review as an investor has to break down business prospects vs. what I am paying for them.

So, at a price of $328 in January 2026, I feel it prudent to downgrade the stock to Hold for a 12-month outlook. I suspect GOOG and GOOGL will move similarly to overall S&P 500 performance for a spell of time. I don't own a position today, as I can find better risk/reward-to-growth rate ideas elsewhere.

Stretched Valuation

Seeking Alpha's present Quant Valuation Grade for Alphabet is an "F" score. The rating may be a little harsh, but the point is shares are not the value play they were a year ago. Statistically speaking, investors buying over $300 do not have the same backing from company operations, including returns/yields on capital invested. Plain and simple logic.

My personal grading of the valuation story at Alphabet/Google would have been in the "B+" area a year ago vs. "C-" today. However you slice it, shares are not the same valuation proposition after the double in its quote.

For a basic valuation approach, I like to use enterprise valuations (which include equity market value plus all net debt on the balance sheet). If you zeroed out debt (paid off all interest-related IOUs) and could acquire Alphabet for yourself at $328 per share, what kind of business returns (cash flow) would you actually get?

Well, EV to EBITDA and sales multiples are the highest since 2007, going into the Great Recession. Had you purchased shares at any other time from 2008 to late 2025, you could have received better underlying returns on investment. Looking at early 2025's opportunity, using forward stat estimates, you could have purchased GOOG/GOOGL at sub-10x EV to EBITDA ratios. Today, the equivalent calculations are 26x trailing and 18x forward numbers.

Using either trailing or forward sales estimates, an EV range on forecast sales of 8.5x to 10x is significantly above long-term averages around 5x.

The worst valuation news is found in the free cash flow yield calculation on the upfront investment. Since AI-datacenters are not really profitable yet, and tens of billions are being invested yearly to build them (by Alphabet), the level of free cash has actually stagnated at the company (since early 2023), compounding the problem of a double in the share quote. FCF yield of 1.8% trailing results is very worrisome vs. rates above 5% in late 2022 or compared to prevailing short-term interest rates in the Treasury bond market. Purchasing a negative -1.7% FCF yield adjusted to alternative and super-safe cash investment vehicles is fraught with risk, assuming AI software models do not provide massive operating profits (rewards on CAPEX investment) sooner rather than later.

But that's not all the bad news on the fundamental valuation front. Price to earnings, divided by earnings growth rates (PEG ratio analysis), highlights a dramatically different core valuation vs. one year ago. While forward PEG multiples were in the 1.5x zone at the price bottom in April 2025, today's estimated annual growth in the 10% to 15% range going forward pushes PEG above 2x to as high as 3x multiples (depending on actual growth rates in 2026-27). I view this valuation as more fully and fairly representative of Alphabet's immediate business outlook.

Fading Upside Momentum

In addition to the overvaluation issues new investors must deal with, chart trading momentum has faded in recent months. 20-day Chaikin Money Flow peaked in September, 13-day Force Index readings topped in November, and 14-day Ease of Movement calculations rolled over sharply in December (all circled in blue below). In fact, EMV readings reached a record low in December after the early month top (circled in gold). This occurrence by itself is a major warning flag that gains in price could end soon, as it may indicate a vacuum in buying power is developing (everyone is in the pool already, so to speak).

In terms of my technical trading outlook for price, something similar to 2007-09 could be Alphabet's future. An eventual AI-bust and U.S. recession will likely keep a lid on share pricing (as my base-case forecast). GOOG/GOOGL owners may see some wild swings with slight outperformance of the S&P 500 in 2026-27, but little overall progress for nominal share pricing.

Final Thoughts

Insiders have been large direct sellers in the open market over the past three weeks, including CEO Sundar Pichai. Like most other Big Tech names in late 2025 and early 2026, managers, directors, and top shareholders have been liquidating positions at a very high rate.

Selling offset by stock option grants has amounted to greater than 4.6 million in net shares liquidated over the latest 12-month period (approaching $1.2 billion in value). I view heavy insider selling as a slight negative generally when predicting any equity quote's direction, all other variables remaining the same.

How could Alphabet/Google continue in an uptrend? Several avenues remain for bulls to earn some money. First, lower interest rates in America and a pickup in worldwide economic growth could produce a higher valuation on even stronger sales/earnings than now forecast. I place odds of this scenario at 20%.

Second, the AI-boom phase in related stocks could stay in place another year. I believe the odds of this happening are not great, maybe a 20% chance. The end result would be an expansion in valuation multiples with further price rallies.

Third, operating results could surprise if the Gemini AI invention clearly becomes the gold standard for large language models, surpassing the first mover OpenAI and its ChatGPT incarnations. Experts believe the latest Gemini version has a list of advantages over ChatGPT. Gemini software, including the newly released version 3, has an estimated 16% market share in the basic AI marketplace during January 2026, which is a distant #2 to leading ChatGPT versions at 73%. Let's give this outcome a 10% chance.

So, the rest of my potential outcomes represent about a 50% chance of flat to bearish investment returns from the stock into year-end 2026. Bearish scenarios include a stock market crash or major Wall Street lull in buying activity, a potential recession in online advertising, a mean reversion for the Alphabet valuation, etc. If a 12-month investment in Alphabet/Google has 50/50 odds of earning an investment profit, why not look at other stocks with smarter odds of success?

In terms of steady growth trends in YouTube and regular Google Search (helped by Gemini interest), on top of the potential for rising growth rates in the Waymo self-driving unit and in-house computer-chip research efforts (including AI-related TPU processing inventions and quantum Willow), Alphabet remains a solid choice for investors to Hold.

For a rating summary - at this stage, I would not increase my position size or initiate a new one. The prudent choice may be to wait for lower share pricing to appear in 2026 if you want to purchase GOOG/GOOGL.

That's my take on the Alphabet/Google investment setup today. I hope this adds some color and perspective to your research. I, personally, have exited a GOOGL stake entirely, as I tend to trade frequently (and focus on undervalued choices).

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: My 21% Yielding Portfolio Update And Lessons Learned
data.description:
Introduction

I wrote about my Combined High-Yield Portfolio For Sustained Income Growth back in October 2024. I have been very busy with other priorities (work and family) and want to apologize for not updating my followers on each change that I have made since. This article attempts to do just that: update you on the changes to this portfolio, including the reasons, lessons learnt, performance figures, risks, and the expectations for the updated portfolio going forward. No fundamental shifts occurred relating to my general approach and selection criteria, which you can read more about here. However, learnings and new investments on offer were the main driving force behind the changes.

The investing-for-income space sure is an exciting space with a plethora of new products being launched on a continuous basis. One can easily get overwhelmed with the choices and potential decisions to be made, not to mention the risks coming along with many of these aiming to maximize yield without having much of a track record.

An additional element I am addressing is to critically think about gradually building up a balanced and diversified portfolio over time. So this update is moving in that direction without trying to claim that this portfolio is the ultimately diversified portfolio meeting all investor requirements. At the end, investor objectives and preferences differ, and there is no one-size-fits-all type of portfolio. I have made great progress, which I am very thankful for, but am still a relatively small investor, so my preferences and objectives are slightly tilted towards a combination of high yield and growth. I also am ok with some volatility, as I believe that it comes with growth investments, but this does not necessarily translate to high risk in the longer term if you focus on quality.

Lessons learned and changes

The intention was always to accumulate assets with growing income streams over the long term with the option to adjust if and where needed. I have learned that investments with not a high enough yield are not going to achieve my investment objectives. This is due to the reality of inflation, which tends to be higher if you compare my typical household expenses with that of official figures. Tax (if any) also needs to be factored in before calculating the real return on any investment.

Capital gains are complicated due to the constantly changing market prices with the reality of actual selling to realize profit; otherwise, it is only paper gains and non-existent. Trying to consistently capture gains in the long run is very tricky and not for everyone. Having said that, I did sell some of my investments to realize a capital gain where I believe that circumstances warrant it, like a change in performance and outperformance from (new) peers or benchmarks - more details about this later as part of the portfolio review.

A more passive approach with only modest to reasonable capital growth to sustain and grow the income over time seems to be what is working best for me. This also helps me to stay focused and resist any urge to sell in a hurry without very sound reasoning: less stock, less income. Once again, long-term investing in quality is the basis. I was always very careful of single company normal dividend stocks due to the inherent risks relative to diversified funds, but I am now even more selective and paying close attention to the risk-reward balance due to the availability of higher yielding ETF options. However, high-yielding covered call-type ETFs cannot be expected to have a meaningfully higher total return than the underlying stock they are based upon. So if the yield is far exceeding the actual or expected total return of the underlying stock, expect meaningful price erosion and the yield to be unsustainable. Lastly, diversifying the currencies in which investments are denominated (not just USD-based) is a good idea, given the current global dynamics. There might be excellent investment opportunities outside the US and the US dollar. Let's take a look at the old portfolio, reasons for changes, and the new portfolio.

Old Portfolio & Changes

Ticker

Comment

BIP

Sold in profit due to overall risk-reward profile reconsideration. Dividend and dividend growth do not support the risk-reward balance for single-company stock when inflation is considered.

DIVB

Was on the watch list. Still not invested here. Dividend and dividend growth not supporting risk-reward balance.

HTAE.CA

Sold in profit due to substantial underperformance in total return relative to QQQ since about a year ago.

HTA.U.CA

Sold in profit due to substantial underperformance in total return relative to QQQ since about a year ago.

JEPQ

Was on the watch list. Still not invested here. Better total return alternatives.

MSFO

Had about the same total return as MSFT since inception but sold in profit due to concern over price erosion and subsequent sustainability of distribution.

PWCDF

Sold in profit due to overall risk-reward profile reconsideration. Dividend and dividend growth do not support the risk-reward balance for single-company stock when inflation is considered.

Updated Portfolio

Here is my updated portfolio with key, well-known performance figures indicated and N/A where the fund is too young to reflect such figures with SPY figures as reference (but not necessarily a benchmark). Please note that the portfolio consists of CAD- and USD-denominated funds. I have ignored any relative movements in these two currencies for the sake of simplicity, but I believe they are still valid for illustration purposes as the differences are fairly small over the indicated periods: six months: hardly any difference, one year: about 4.8% strengthening of CAD, and two years: about 1.7% weakening of CAD (at the time of writing). This portfolio is still in the making and not fully diversified yet. Source: Seeking Alpha at market close on January 23rd, 2026:

The very high 21.04% average TTM yield of the portfolio is supported by average total return figures consistently beating SPY over the three different time frames. A number of these ETFs are still young but do provide some assurance that the portfolio is not just about yield but total return and sustainability as well. Continuous research and monitoring are being done to carefully expand this portfolio for diversity and sustainability with top quality ETFs.

Brief Reasons for Stock Selection in the Updated Portfolio

BANK:CA is tapping into the great investment opportunity that is Canadian Financials, consisting of the "big six" Canadian banks and four of the largest Canadian Life Co's. These Canadian banks have consistently grown dividends stretching back into the 19th century, which provide a good foundation for the distribution. Although they are Canadian, operations are generally global with growing operations across North America, South America, and the Far East. The Evolve ETF fund managers seem to be doing a great job with covered call writing. The distribution has been increased four times over the four years of its existence.

UTES:CA Also managed by Evolve ETFs, this is a defensive play with a strong dividend-paying portfolio in the utilities, pipelines, and telecoms space. The trailing twelve months yield average for its constituents is 5% with averages of around 2.7% and 3.5% for the three- and five-year dividend growth rates, respectively. This is (like BANK:CA) supplemented with covered calls to boost the yield. I do not expect big growth here, but rather sustainable, slow growth.

I have written a recent article on AMZP addressing the investment thesis for this top 21% yielding Amazon ETF. In essence, AMZN is a top growth mega cap with huge earnings growth fueled by its diverse and growing number of revenue-generating businesses. AMZP is a covered call type income fund with more than two years of history and outperforming peers. Amazon's value metrics are also looking the best in years, further supporting future price growth.

It was tricky to choose between NVHE.U and NVII with the 17-month-old NVHE.U having a longer track record than the eight-month-old (but better total returning) NVII and decided to invest in both to manage risk across the two fund managers. Both are outperforming other peers and are a practical way of deriving consistent income from the success of NVDA without trying to time the market. Based on earnings estimates, growth prospects for NVDA are exceptional for the foreseeable future, and NVDA has beaten estimates consistently for at least a year. Such exceptional growth could provide sustainability for these two high-yielding ETFs.

Risks to the portfolio

The raging tariff war is a headwind for the portfolio with impacts on the financial, utilities, technology, and consumer discretionary sectors represented here not to be denied. However, resilience is displayed throughout these sectors. Income ETFs based on single company stocks are not ideal from a diversification perspective but do provide for selectivity and exclusion of unwanted companies. The high volatility of the technology sector could impact the consistent performance of covered call type funds. Conversely, the technology sector is one of the best growth points currently driven by the acceleration of the fourth industrial revolution, which could sustain high-yielding covered call type funds. As mentioned, this portfolio is still a work in progress with some relatively young ETFs and is not yet fully diversified, with potentially less resilience during market shocks. Lastly, the inclusion of CAD-denominated ETFs does introduce forex risk; however, this is a double-edged sword and also a practical way to diversify USD exposure.

Why the portfolio should continue to deliver

The interesting balance between long-standing dividend growth from Canadian financials, utilities, pipelines, and telecoms on the one hand and superior growth from selected companies driven by the acceleration of the fourth industrial revolution should provide a solid foundation for income-focused ETFs with sustainable and even growing high yield. The portfolio is comprised of ETFs based again on large- to mega-cap companies with proven business models and long earnings growth histories, which most probably should continue for the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

It is possible to build a quality, high-yield portfolio of income-focused ETFs beating inflation as well as the S&P 500 in total return with manageable risk. This could be obtained by utilizing a combination of opportunities across old, trusted business models, country boundaries, and various sectors.

data.title: PMI Continues To Be Made Irrelevant By Reality
data.description:
Industrial capacity continues to rise, as does industrial production, both revised higher. The standout is the PMI remaining below 50, now ~40 months mostly below 50, reflecting an intractable period of market pessimism. Even with the rises we have seen in selected industrial issues, I see a remarkable period still ahead of heady price movement once market pessimism converts to recognition of economic expansion.

The current administration’s tariff policies have yet to see the initial building of additional capacity that the reported $10-18 trillion implies. There remain 3-4 years of strong GDP growth ahead, in my opinion, with a decent period of increased wage growth, retail sales and expansion of individual home ownership.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IBB: Beyond The 2025 Surge, Biotech Breakout
data.description:
After a multi-year drought, the iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) has been quietly having a fantastic run, returning about 28% for 2025, dwarfing its modest returns in 2023, alongside losses in 2022, 2024, amid a renewed risk appetite, a healthy environment of IPO & M&A activity, alongside a sturdy show by large-cap biotechs, where IBB derives its weights from.

As we move forward, 2026 looks like a promising year, given the number of catalysts within the biotechs world that investors keep a close eye on, growing M&A activity, alongside a release of FDA authorizations, and an increasing use of AI technologies that will fast-track biotechs timelines, benefiting many of IBB's constituents. Unlike some of its peers, such as XBI, which is equal-weighted across its portfolio and as a consequence offers riskier exposure to smaller companies, IBB's focus on the cash-flow-positive giants such as Amgen (AMGN), Vertex (VRTX), and Gilead (GILD) creates a "safe" entry into the innovation cycle, given its ability to better manage idiosyncratic risk.

The Fund

iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) is a passive fund seeking to replicate the performance of the NYSE Biotechnology Index. Key details about the index are that it is market cap weighted, free float adjusted, and quarterly rebalanced. The number of constituents is variable, while constituents must have a market cap of at least $200 million to be included in the index, an average daily traded volume in the last three months of at least 100 thousand shares, and the maximum weighting is set at 9% for an individual issuer, and the total weighting of issuers with at least 4.5% weight cannot be over 36%. Given these criteria, the fund cannot have a severely concentrated structure. You can read up more on the methodology of the index here. The number of holdings currently is 258, the fund inception date is February 5, 2001, and the fund AUM is $8.65 billion, defining it as a large fund in terms of assets and with a pretty long track record dating back to 2001.

We will use Seeking Alpha's grading system to do an overview of the fund characteristics and compare it to peer funds. The fund possesses a Seeking Alpha B- Expense Grade, with a 0.44% expense ratio and a Bid/Ask % Spread alone graded A. Regarding liquidity, the fund has a Seeking Alpha A+ Grade, with superior daily liquidity, an average of 2.07 million shares traded daily over the last three months, and the average volume being around $345 million. Assets under management, as we previously mentioned, are fairly huge compared to the median fund. The trailing dividend yield is 0.22%, with a decreasing dividend growth rate of -0.44%. This, along with the other dividend metrics covered by the Seeking Alpha grading system, earns the fund a Dividend Grade of B-.

Compared to biotech peers, we could say that IBB holds comparatively well, generally lagging slightly in the Momentum category, while having better than average Expenses, Dividends, and Liquidity grades. The expense ratios differ between the funds, with Invesco Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF (IBBQ) having the lowest of the group with 0.19% and the highest being 0.54% charged by First Trust NYSE Arca Biotechnology Index Fund ETF (FBT). IBB comes in the middle with a 0.44% expense ratio.

Summing up, the difference between the groups is that IBB is the most diversified with 258 constituents, while State Street® SPDR® S&P® Biotech ETF (XBI) and FBT have fewer holdings with around 150 and 30, respectively. IBB is market cap weighted, so the fund naturally tilts towards larger companies, while XBI and FBT impose an equal weighting methodology.

Biotech in 2026 and Beyond

The primary catalyst for the biotech group to move higher by the end of 2026 relates to the acceleration of strategic deals, with the amount expected to rise by an additional 15% to an outstanding $230 billion, according to ING. As large-cap companies in the pharmaceutical industry encounter the patent cliff, which is expected to see an additional $200 to $250 billion in sales threatened with generic competition by the end of 2030, the imperative to fill the pipelines is dire, resulting in a series of high-premium transactions to purchase small to mid-cap biotech companies. Recent activity, including the Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) purchase of Intra-Cellular Therapies, or the acquisition by Novartis (NVS) of Avidity Biosciences, suggests the premiums paid for de-risked Phase III assets are likely to be quite high indeed.

A linked catalyst is the continued strength in FDA approvals. In 2024, the FDA approved approximately 50 new therapeutics, with oncology and orphan indications heavily represented, and 2025 has maintained a strong pace of novel drug approvals with 46 new drugs approved, further reinforcing the notion that the regulatory environment remains constructive for innovation-driven biotechs.

Monetary policy is a major catalyst for the biotech sector. With most market participants anticipating a move towards the 3.0%-3.5% range in the Fed Funds rate, growing weakness in the labor market and subdued inflation could prompt the Fed to cut rates more. In my opinion, and this shall be a major argument for the thesis, this is very much likely. The labor market has certainly been weakened the last few quarters, with Non-Farm Payrolls showing a declining trend. This, in combination with the deflationary effects of AI, could give the Fed leeway to cut rates more than the market consensus, potentially leading to interest rates to 2.5% or lower.

In addition, the leadership change at the Fed due this spring offers a dovish moment possibility as the next Fed Chair could be even friendlier to economic growth and Fed interest rate reductions than markets currently perceive.

In the case of IBB, this monetary easing environment represents a valuation multiplier. In fact, biotech is a "long duration" asset class, meaning the majority of its value is 5-10 years out. Biotech is extremely sensitive to the discount rates used in valuation models. In fact, it reduces the negative impact of high capital costs on a pre-revenue entity and simultaneously decreases the funding hurdle for big pharmaceutical companies' M&A.

Additionally, the long-term investment thesis is concentrated on two pillars: the AI integration in discoveries and next-generation modalities. Notably, AI is actively compressing R&D cycles anywhere from 25% to 50%. The total market opportunity in the space of AI in drug discovery is expected to rise at a CAGR of nearly 25% until 2033. At the same time, we are witnessing the lab-to-clinic transition for the in vivo CRISPR and in vivo CAR-T therapies, moving the industry away from chronic treatment models to high-margin interventions.

Valuation

From a valuation standpoint, trading at a P/E multiple of around 19.01x is surprisingly reasonable when compared to the broader market and the tech sector. The P/S multiple of around 4.07x remains high as investors are willing to pay for high-margin biological intellectual property. We can also see that IBB trades below the other covered multiples relative to the broader healthcare category average.

The fund looks good from a technical perspective, hovering above the 20-, 50-, and 200-Day MA.

Risks

The investment thesis isn't without its "side effects." Firstly, the fund is concentrated in terms of holdings, with around 48% being invested in the top 10 holdings. This means that clinical trial failures or regulatory setbacks for the big titans in the fund can have a severe impact on the extended industry and the fund as a whole. Furthermore, despite the stabilization of the current regulatory environment, the second round of Medicare-negotiated price cuts under the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) could potentially pressure margins and disrupt the industry sentiment. Sentiment can swing sharply, and potential investors should be wary of event risk- regulatory and FDA approval risk.

Conclusion

iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB), in my opinion, presents a balanced entry point to the biotech sector for 2026, with the stability of established heavyweights in the industry having the highest weightings in the fund. With the trifecta of a possible more dovish Fed policy, AI-backed R&D efficiencies, accelerating M&A, and continued strength in FDA approvals, the biotech sector, in my opinion, is positioned to benefit. While being mindful of top-heavy concentration and regulatory headwinds, IBB's valuation as well as technical positioning paired with the catalysts point me to rate it a BUY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: REIT Replay: U.S. REIT Indexes Decline During Week Ended Jan. 23
data.description:
Indexes tracking US equity real estate investment trusts declined during the week ended Jan. 23.

The Dow Jones Equity All REIT Index (REI:IND) fell 2.36% over the recent week, while the MSCI US REIT (RMZ) index also dropped 2.26%.

The broader stock market indexes also logged small declines, with the S&P 500 down 0.35% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) down 0.53%.

All of the Dow Jones US real estate property sector indexes also closed the recent week in the red. The industrial and self-storage REIT indexes logged the largest declines, down 3.89% and 3.77%, respectively, followed by the healthcare REIT index with a decline of 2.98%.

Industrial REIT Industrial Logistics Properties Trust recorded the largest share-price decline among all US equity REITs with at least $200 million in market capitalization, down 6.21%. Ground lease-oriented Safehold Inc. (SAFE) logged the second-largest share-price decline this past week, down 5.74%. Three office REITs followed next to round out the bottom-five performing REIT stocks. Hudson Pacific Properties Inc.'s (HPP) share price dropped 5.62% over the week, while Kilroy Realty Corp. (KRC) and SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) both recorded share-price drops of 5.60%.

On the other end, NexPoint Diversified Real Estate Trust (NXDT) logged the largest share-price increase during the week, up 9.11%. Net lease-focused Alpine Income Property Trust Inc. (PINE) and hotel REIT Xenia Hotels & Resorts Inc. (XHR) rounded out the top three with share price increases of 3.82% and 3.61%, respectively.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Strattec Security: Hidden Value In An Overlooked Auto Supplier
data.description:
The technological market is every day more incorporated into the automotive sector, but the issue of access and its endless possibilities is, I believe, not fully explored. And much more since the development of AI-advanced software, which could create sliding doors and smart keys inside my own cellphone. If you think of electric and autonomous cars, how not to think about doors with facial recognition or fingerprints?

It's precisely in this market where I believe Strattec Security Corporation (STRT) is an ideal option to invest in for its specialization in electric vehicle access. This specialization sets STRT apart from other large companies that sell mass-market automotive electronics, such as Lear Corporation (LEA) or Magna International (MGA). In both cases, the market capitalization far exceeds STRT.

One of the factors I liked the most about STRT is its long-term revenue growth profile. I connected this with other key factors: the stage of growth in scale that the company is in, on the one hand, and the market in which it operates, on the other.

First Catalyst: A Rapidly Scaling Growth Investment

In my view, the company is in the phase of plain growth. Revenue increased from $493 million in 2023 to $565 million in 2025. Including Q1 FY26 (September 2025), growth continues at a good pace. You can see in the following image not only the sales growth but also the cash accumulation and the expansion of profit margins.

As a complement, you can see in the following chart other key operational metrics that show an upward trend: EBIT, net income, and free cash flow. You can also see the EPS growth, which, after being negative in 2023, has steadily increased. In the last three years, it has doubled its average earnings per share.

And I think this is just the beginning. I consider this to be part of the growth in scale. Part of this confidence comes from its prestigious client portfolio, despite being a small capitalization company. 64% of its sales are to General Motors (GM), Ford (F), and Stellantis (STLA), which gives me security regarding its more solid clients.

Why do I consider that the growth can go on? Because the growth projections are very favorable, if we take the same metrics, especially EBIT, EBITDA, and earnings per share.

The question is then: why are the projections so interesting? My first answer is that this is an expanding market with little specialized competition in what STRT does within the United States. Let's analyze this industry.

Second Catalyst: An Expanding Market

The vehicle access control market, as I mentioned at the beginning, is booming, with a projected CAGR of 11.7% globally through 2030. Biometric and non-biometric systems will promote the adoption of this technology. But the strongest growth is being seen in North America, tripling global growth: the projected CAGR rate until 2030 is 33.5%.

In Asia Pacific, the industry will also maintain a strong growth rate of 14.6%, but it is still not approaching North America. I believe this is key because STRT left the Chinese market in 2023 because the company was seeing geopolitical risks, which implied a kind of anticipation of the tariff problem of April 2025. Other competitors, such as LEA, more focused on China, are projecting offshoring strategies to avoid higher production costs.

Anyway, Strattec isn't free from tariff challenges, because a great part of its assembly is done in Mexico. However, the USMCA, although it was part of President Donald Trump's criticisms, still provides exemptions for industrial production. That's what CEO Jennifer Slater said in December.

Most of our customers, this (tariffs) is facing their entire supply base. So they've worked with suppliers to make sure that they've got a process for reimbursement. And obviously, also working with the supply base to make sure that long term, suppliers have a plan to reduce any tariff impact.

The fact that we are majority USMCA certified helps us with that because we don't have a tariff impact on our final assemblies from USMCA. We do have some components that come from other regions, and we're working longer term with our customers on what that -- how we would support a transition to more local supply.

Presents at iAccess Alpha Virtual Best Ideas Winter Investment Conference 2025 Transcript

What I wanted to show with this quote and with the context of tariffs in general is that STRT was right in concentrating its production in North America because it is the region with the highest expected long-term growth rate for its segment. We must remember that STRT is not a giant in the automotive equipment industry, so a misstep that increases its costs could be very detrimental.

But precisely because it is not a giant in the automotive sector, that's where I see its greatest potential. The report from Grand View Research, which I showed above, does not place STRT among the top 10 brands in the industry.

As there is a lot of market share to win, I believe the company could capture more and more clients based on its intelligent vehicle access control segment. To make a comparison that is more accurate with my thesis (that this market does not have large specialized players,) I investigated in depth its competitors in the industry (automotive parts and equipment).

When I say STRT won't dispute a large amount of competition, I'm saying that just LEA and MGA are its most direct competitors. Let's also see two German companies. Continental AG (CTTAF), which develops secure digital keys, and HELLA (HLKHF), which develops smart door sensors. Robert Bosch is another competitor, but it is a private company.

Finally, I added Gentex Corporation (GNTX) to my analysis, which is dedicated to the technological link between the vehicle and the home. I also thought about incorporating Dorman Products (DORM), which offers lock replacements on a massive scale, but this market only represents 8% of STRT's sales. In other words, STRT's business is not to sell its products to drivers but to big manufacturers; that's why I can't rule out Dorman.

Based on this market analysis, I made a chart with each company mentioned, marking their growth projections with the corresponding market capitalization. STRT is the best of them in comparison. But let's not only focus on the comparison, as that could be random. My point is there isn't a direct competitor to STRT relative to its size. LEA and MGN, as I said before, have a much higher market cap and revenue.

Q2 FY26 Forecast

Q2 FY26 results will be presented on February 5th, so profits are just around the corner. The earnings per share estimate sets a target of $0.93, which would represent a YoY growth of 43%. We can't expect revenue to grow at the same rate: just 1.8% until reaching $132 million. The company has been quietly meeting consensus estimates in recent quarters, in line with its good operating performance.

The feelings of the last result presentation (Q1 FY26) were very cautious regarding the short-term forecasts due to a fire at an aluminum supplier and some restrictions on international semiconductor trade. Management announced that it would take the opportunity to strengthen its inventories to avoid changes in future forecasts.

Let's remember there was a small crisis in the automotive sector in the last quarter of 2025 due to an increase in the default rate on car loans. The deteriorated industry expectations, in my opinion. But Q2 FY results are more important because of the future forecasts. Although I hope STRT will exceed the consensus estimates, my investment is intended for the long term.

Intrinsic Value Under a Conservative Scenario

I consider the low market capitalization of the company and the rise in the share price in the last year (+102%) may have been factors leading to ignoring the analysis of the vehicle access control market. The last analysts here on Seeking Alpha who wrote about the company rated it as a Hold, not because of operational problems but because of a combination of lower forecasts and an expensive premium reflected in the stock.

But, on the contrary, I don't see the lower forecasts affecting profitability growth or earnings per share. And regarding the valuation, the ratios compared to the sector are showing me that it's undervalued. The forward P/E ratio is 14.1x versus 19.8x, to give an example.

But to deeply analyze the company's potential based on its revenue growth and as a consequence of capturing a larger share of its market, I developed a DCF model. Anyway, I've tried to be very conservative, especially regarding the income growth rate. The operating income growth rate over the last three years was 54.61%, an increase explained by the boost from a small base in fiscal year 2022, at $8.4 million. So, I wanted to be careful in my estimation, based on the growth rate of the last ten years, which was 2.59%. Following this estimation, I started with an operating income of $28.6 (the number of the last 12 months) and closed the model at $34.7 million.

I used a perpetual growth rate of 3%, in line with expected global growth, a WACC of 9.3%, and a tax rate of 21.7%. For D&A and CAPEX, I maintained a similar pace to that recorded in recent years, slightly increasing the latter metric due to investments in technology for intelligent systems.

So, to conclude, I believe my model was very conservative, with a moderate projection of an operating income. The result gave me an upside of 37%, which I think is very attractive, because any higher growth, as I anticipate in my investment thesis, could reflect a greater momentum in the share price.

Macroeconomic and Trade Risks

The stock also has its risks, despite its good growth projections and the potential I see for it to gain an increasingly larger position in its market. One of those risks is the concentration in three big factories: General Motors, Ford, and Stellantis. Although they are not leading brands, I'd like to see a greater diversification in the future, to not compromise growth due to the financial difficulties of any of these companies.

On the other hand, the macroeconomic context is very important to Strattec, starting with tariffs and the possible increase in manufacturing costs. In this case, the UMSAC is the commercial guarantee for the assembly plants that the company has in Mexico. Trump is expected to review the treaty in the middle of this year. Another macroeconomic factor is the interest rate environment, since, due to the company's size, a high interest rate can affect its balance sheets. However, the company has a very healthy debt profile, which reduces the risk of financial complications should rates rise, an unlikely scenario for 2026.

Summary

The automotive sector has an internal market that's combining advanced technology in the equipment and parts of the vehicles. The subsector of keys and vehicle access is where I believe a company like Strattec Security can profit. The Internet of Things, AI, and biometric sensors are taking the experience of vehicles to the next level. I believe this is a very good time to invest in this market. If you didn't know it, I consider STRT a very good option at an affordable price, with a lot of market share to gain and with interesting projections for future growth.

data.title: Kroger: Value Investment In An Expensive Market
data.description:
Kroger (KR) is back on my radar for an investment, trading at a reasonable valuation of 13.4x forward P/E and looking relatively safe as a consumer staples business in an expensive stock market. Despite the recent write-off involving the automated fulfillment network driving the share price lower, that realism by management should, in my opinion, help Kroger focus on delivering value to both customers and shareholders by focusing on getting back to basics.

With less emphasis on e-commerce efforts, Kroger should have more cash to return to shareholders in the coming years, which will help to continue to grow its 2.2% dividend in addition to strong annual share repurchases, which will be discussed more later. With Kroger now 14.2% off 52-week highs, this consumer staples company is starting to look appealing again in an expensive market.

Since I last wrote about Kroger back in February 2021, the company has had a total return, including dividends, around 105% compared to the S&P 500's total return of 75%. Since this time period roughly five years ago, Kroger has also lowered its share count a solid 17.1% from 790 to 655 million shares. I have been a shareholder in and out of Kroger over the years since the 30% fall back in 2017 from the overreaction to when Amazon purchased Whole Foods and before Berkshire Hathaway invested in the business in 2020, which sent the shares up sharply. After the recent pullback, Kroger's valuation is looking enticing again, especially in the expensive stock market these days.

Latest Q3 2025 Results

Kroger last released Q3 2025 earnings on December 4th, reporting mixed results with slowing growth, with identical sales up a healthy 2.6% (excluding fuel) and adjusted EPS of $1.05 for the quarter. The unadjusted GAAP figures showed an operating loss of $1,541 million and EPS of negative $2.02, which need to be taken with a grain of salt as they include $2.6 billion ($3.00 loss per share) of impairment and related charges related to the automated fulfillment network. These latest results bring the full year 2025 adjusted EPS guidance to $4.77 for around a 13.4x forward P/E and an earnings yield of 7.5%.

E-commerce sales increased 17%, which, along with the strategic review and impairment charge, means management now expects they will make the eCommerce business profitable in 2026. Whether or not this forecast from management will turn out to be accurate, it is refreshing to see Kroger's management segmenting the business realistically and taking such a hard look at it in order to get the full perspective of capital expenditures back on track.

In the updated full-year guidance provided by management in the Q3 results are identical sales without fuel of 2.8%–3.0% and free cash flow guidance of $2.8 to $3.0 billion after capital expenditures of $3.6–$3.8 billion. Putting the midpoint of this FCF guidance into yield form, we get a forward FCF yield of 6.8%. We will take a look at some more historical valuation approaches as well for conservatism.

Great Company That Continues Growing

The recent impairment headlines may obscure the great cash flow and profitability characteristics of Kroger to short-term investors. However, Kroger's national scale and operations have allowed the company to achieve an impressive long-term average return on equity and return on invested capital of 24.3% and 10.6%, respectively, since 2008. This level of profitability includes all GAAP items, even unfavorable ones such as the latest large write-off regarding the automated fulfillment network, as mistakes will happen even in great companies with good economic moats. Even over the most recent 5 years, when the latest impairments carry a bigger weighting, the ROE averages out to a solid 20.9%. As we will see later, though, when we discuss valuation, the average ROE needs to be considered along with the price investors are paying for that equity in the markets.

Over this time period, dividend growth has been on par with my expectations for a mature consumer staples company. From pre-COVID FY 2018 to the TTM period, the dividend has grown from $0.53/share to $1.31 for an average compound growth rate around 13.8%. The forward-looking $1.40 per share dividend looks well supported by the $4.77 midpoint of management's adjusted forward earnings with an implied 29.4% dividend payout ratio (excluding the e-commerce write-down). This low payout ratio leaves room for future dividend growth and more share repurchases to continue. Let's analyze the potential for increased dividends more through looking at the cash flows.

Great Cash Flows at a Good Price

Kroger does a good job of generating cash and returning it to shareholders through both dividends and share repurchases. To get an idea of the sustainability of dividends and share repurchases, we can take a look at what percentage of cash flow from operations is available to be returned to shareholders after making the necessary capital expenditures, estimated by management to be a significant $3.7 billion in FY 2025.

As can be seen below, capital expenditures and acquisitions only used up on average only 56% of cash flow from operations over the past five years. This leaves approximately 44% to be returned to investors in the form of dividends and share repurchases. With an average cash flow from operations of $5.8 billion over the past five-year period, this 44% would imply free cash flow to shareholders of $2.6 billion for around a 6.1% free cash flow yield at the current $42.6 billion market capitalization.

What does Growth Look Like for Kroger?

Kroger has a good growth story for a consumer staples company with long-term revenue growth since 2007 averaging around 4.2% annualized to bring TTM revenues to $147.2 billion, up from $70.2 billion in 2007. The growth figures are even more impressive on a per-share basis due to the annual share buybacks from the company. Kroger has grown EPS at an impressive average rate of 9.3% since 2007, with EPS (adjusted for the $3.00 per share e-commerce write-down) growing from $0.84 per share to around $4.13 per share, as can be seen in the graph below. Dividend growth has been even higher than these revenue gains at an average rate of 13.8% due to the low payout ratio to begin the period. On a cash flow from operations basis, the TTM 12% dividend payout ratio (52% when share buybacks are included) shows room for continued outsized growth in the dividend compared to EPS.

We should also compare Kroger's historical average growth rates to what could theoretically be able to be achieved sustainably. A company's sustainable growth rate is the growth that can be achieved without changing the capital structure of the business, which makes sense for a mature business such as Kroger. Using the more conservative ROE of 20.9% over the past five years discussed earlier and an adjusted dividend and stock buyback payout ratio of 52%, Kroger's sustainable growth rate could be calculated at 10.0% (20.9% ROE x [100% - 52% payout rate]).

The SGR is quite high and shows the competitive moat of Kroger as a national grocery. The SGR of 10.0% is also right around the long-term historical EPS growth rate mentioned earlier (revenue growth 4.2% and EPS growing 9.3%). Seeing this SGR calculation helps me get comfortable adding the historical EPS growth on top of the pre-growth earnings yields. I would be hesitant to add the full SGR, as that higher growth rate has not been achieved historically. We will take this long-term growth rate and add it on top of the other shareholder yields we have discussed in this article.

Getting a Sense of Valuation

Kroger's 13.4x forward P/E ratio can also be expressed as a 7.5% earnings yield, but I also always like to examine the relationship between average ROE and price-to-book value in what I call the Investors' Adjusted ROE. Investors' Adjusted ROE examines the average ROE over a business cycle and adjusts that ROE for the price investors are currently paying for the company's book value or equity per share.

With Kroger earning an average ROE of 20.9% over the past decade and shares currently trading at a price-to-book value of 4.54x when the price is $64.25, this would yield an Investors' Adjusted ROE of 4.6% for an investors' equity at that purchase price if history repeats itself. This is well below the 9% that I like to see, but we could add the average of 9.3% historical EPS growth or the 10% sustainable growth rate discussed earlier to increase the potential total return up to 13.9% or 14.6%, respectively. Below are potential yields graphed for Kroger, including adding the SGR growth rate discussed earlier and a more conservative 3% growth rate to represent Kroger growing alongside the general economy.

Financial Leverage Looks Conservative

Kroger's financial leverage has stayed fairly conservative for the five years, as can be seen in the graph below. Currently, financial leverage sits at a fairly healthy 4.81x today, and the company's interest coverage ratio is a healthy 6.4x in the trailing twelve-month period when adjusting operating profit for the $2.6 billion impairment of the automated fulfillment network.

Kroger has also bought back on average 4.1% of their outstanding shares each year on average since 2007, as can be seen in the graph above. This has helped drive the higher EPS growth compared to the overall 4.2% revenue gains mentioned earlier. Along with Kroger's 2.2% dividend, these 4.1% average share repurchases would indicate total shareholder and cash yields around 6.3%, which is similar to the calculated yields. Share buybacks at Kroger have formed a core part of the company's capital allocation policy, which should help drive long-term EPS growth above same-store sales growth, which is more closely linked to inflation for such a mature company.

Investor Takeaway

Kroger is a great consumer staples company that still trades at a reasonable valuation of 13.4x P/E and offers an enticing 2.20% dividend in addition to regular share repurchases. The recent $2.6 billion impairment ($3.00 loss per share) related to the automated fulfillment network offers a buying opportunity for value investors as well as a hard lesson for management to focus on their core competencies in brick-and-mortar grocery sales.
